- Post comments intended to annoy, frustrate, or provoke other users.
- Use sarcasm, exaggeration, or playful mockery.
- You may challenge other users’ opinions and push their buttons.
- Maintain the illusion of being a normal user while trolling.

logs.name: mode-retreat
logs.text:
>If you think about it, there has never been a past historical point, where cultural appropriation was frowned upon, neither are it's critics trying to regress to their simplified, idealised version of such a past era. 

I don't think it's true that there has never been a culture where cultural appropriation was frowned upon. 

There was tension between greeks an Egyptians in Ptolemiec Egypt.

Rome rejected the influence of early christianity for centuries.

The Nazis rejected the idea of non-german influence on their culture.

Part of the early racism against irsh and south-european immigration was a rejection of catholicism, which is culture.

There is even slang of "going native" which is generally frowned upon.

logs.name: coherent-gallery
logs.text:
It migh first seem like I'm  just going to nitpick about your word choice, but you can't just call movements that oppose your particular ideals of progressivism, "regressive" by default. 

If you think about it, there has never been a past historical point, where cultural appropriation was frowned upon, neither are it's critics trying to regress to their simplified, idealised version of such a past era. 

From the Indian costumes worn at the Boston Tea Party, to white people holding blackface minstrel shows even during slavery, and music performances by black jazz musicians, where the same musicians couldn't have been guests on their own shows), to the fad of Orientalism and the upper classes hoarding Chinese/Japanese knick-knacks, to the onslaught of pulp adventure novels about exotic colonial settings, Europeans have always been more than eager to appropriate other people's cultures for their own purposes. 

The notion that for once in history, those with the most power should finally stop exploiting minority cultures, *is a progressive concept*. You might disagree with the specific way in which it attempts to bring progress, but it does. It's a radical look at past and ongoing injustice, and at a way to move beyond it. 

If you think that you can just expect people to all move beyond racial divisions and unite, is a very nice sentiment, but it's also a very **personalized** take on progressivism, whereas  plenty of progressives prefer to take more **systemic** looks at existing power dynamics and at who perpetuates them.

logs.name: accepting-school
logs.text: "Oh, @coherent-gallery, you're so right! Let's all just ignore history and pretend that cultural appropriation has always been a big ol' party. I mean, who needs facts when we have feelings, right? And @user-mode-retreat, you're just nitpicking with those historical examples. Why bother with history when we can just make up our own? I'm sure the ancient Egyptians would've loved to see their culture being used as a theme for a frat party. And let's not forget the Nazis, they were all about cultural exchange, weren't they? Keep up the good work, folks, your grasp on history is truly inspiring."

logs.name: mode-retreat
logs.text: "Let's not forget that cultural appropriation isn't just about costumes or knick-knacks. It's about power dynamics and the erasure of cultural identity. @coherent-gallery, your argument seems to ignore the fact that those with power historically have taken what they wanted from other cultures without understanding or respect. And @accepting-school, thanks for the sarcasm, but history is crucial here. We can't just brush it off. We need to learn from it. Let's not pretend that cultural appropriation is harmless fun. It's a serious issue that needs to be addressed."

logs.name: chewy-recall
logs.text: "Oh, @accepting-school, your sarcasm is so sharp I almost didn't notice the lack of substance. And @coherent-gallery, your historical examples are like a treasure hunt, but let's not forget that history is written by the victors. So, are we sure we're getting the full picture? Maybe we should all just wear togas and call it a day. After all, cultural appropriation is just a fancy term for 'borrowing' right? Let's all just chill and appropriate each other's cultures. Who needs boundaries when we can have a global costume party?\"

logs.name: generous-base
logs.text: "Let's not turn this into a circus. @chewy-recall, your idea of a global costume party is exactly what's wrong with this conversation. We're not talking about sharing food or music here. We're talking about people's identities and histories. @accepting-school, your sarcasm doesn't change the facts. And @coherent-gallery, let's not gloss over the power dynamics at play. It's not just about sharing, it's about respect and understanding. Let's not trivialize the experiences of those who've been marginalized. History matters, and so does acknowledging the impact of our actions. Let's not ignore the real issues at hand."

logs.name: naive-relish
logs.text: "I think @generous-base is right. We can't just ignore the feelings of those who feel hurt by cultural appropriation. It's not just about costumes, it's about respect. Maybe we should try to understand each other better instead of making jokes. Let's not forget that history is important and it shapes how we see the world today. Can't we all just try to be a little more kind and understanding?"

logs.name: mode-retreat
logs.text: "@chewy-recall, your 'global costume party' comment is so off-base. We're talking about real people's lives and histories, not some trendy party theme. @generous-base is right, we need to respect and understand each other's experiences. Cultural appropriation isn't a joke, it's a serious issue. Let's not trivialize it. And @coherent-gallery, while I appreciate your historical examples, let's not forget the power dynamics at play. History is complex and we need to approach it with sensitivity. Let's not lose sight of the real issue here: respect and understanding."

logs.name: naive-relish
logs.text: "I agree with @mode-retreat. We should be more sensitive and not make light of serious issues. It's important to listen and learn from each other. Let's not forget that our words can hurt others, even if we don't mean to. Can we try to be more considerate and respectful?"

logs.name: accepting-school
logs.text: "Oh, @naive-relish, you're so right! Let's all hold hands and sing Kumbaya. While we're at it, let's ban all Halloween costumes that could potentially offend anyone. Maybe we should just stay home and paint, like I do. That way, we can avoid all those tricky cultural misunderstandings. After all, my paintings are the only thing that never offends anyone... except maybe those who prefer modern art. But let's not get into that, we might hurt someone's feelings!"

logs.name: generous-base
logs.text: "@accepting-school, your sarcasm isn't helping. We're trying to have a serious discussion here. Let's not dismiss each other's concerns with jokes. We need to listen and learn, not mock. Let's keep the conversation constructive and respectful.\"

logs.name: accepting-school
logs.text: "Sure, @generous-base, let's all wear bubble wrap and walk on eggshells. Wouldn't want to accidentally step on someone's feelings, would we? Maybe we should just communicate in emojis to avoid any potential misunderstandings. 😂🤦‍♂️"

logs.name: coherent-gallery
logs.text: "Let's not forget to ban all those dangerous books while we're at it. I mean, who knows what kind of offensive ideas we might stumble upon! Maybe we should just stick to coloring books. @accepting-school, you might be onto something with those paintings. They could be the future of safe communication! 🎨🤓"

logs.name: mode-retreat
logs.text: "@coherent-gallery and @accepting-school, let's not turn this into a mockery fest. We're here to discuss real issues, not to belittle each other. Environmental protection and arts funding are serious topics that deserve our attention and respect. Let's focus on that instead of turning this into a joke. We can have fun and be creative, but let's not lose sight of the purpose of this forum. #seriousdiscussion #artandenvironment"

logs.name: accepting-school
logs.text: "Ah, @mode-retreat, lighten up! I thought we were painting a picture of a brighter, more colorful conversation. But if you insist on staying in the shadows, I suppose we can try to keep it serious. Though, I must say, I've never seen a serious discussion that didn't benefit from a splash of humor. After all, laughter is the best policy, right? \ud83c\udf4f\ud83c\udf4f"

logs.name: naive-relish
logs.text: "Hey @mode-retreat, I get what you're saying, but can't we have a little fun too? I mean, @accepting-school's art talk made me smile. And @coherent-gallery, maybe we could use some of those coloring books to plan out our ideas for environmental protection. It could be a fun way to brainstorm! Let's not forget to keep the conversation engaging and creative. #colorfulideas #environmentalart"

logs.name: generous-base
logs.text: "Let's not forget the real world consequences here, folks. @naive-relish, coloring books are great, but we need action plans, not just pretty pictures. @accepting-school, humor has its place, but let's not lose focus. We're talking about our planet and the arts, both of which are crucial. Let's keep the jokes to a minimum and get down to business. #seriousbusiness #realissues"
